Happy Alien Day!

  • Thread starter TristramEvans
  • Start date Apr 26, 2017

TristramEvans

The Right Hand of Doom
Administrator
Moderator
Joined
Apr 24, 2017
Messages
40,398
Reaction score
124,996
  • Apr 26, 2017
  • #1
In honour of yet another superfluous, pop-culture-based holiday thats showing up on my Facebook streams, here's a general question for everyone: what is your favourite depiction of extraterrestrials in an RPG or gameline? Could be a single species you really like, an RPG adaption of an alien from film, or just a general attitude towards aliens an RPG takes.
 
Reactions: Voros, Doc Sammy and dragoner

Shipyard Locked

New optimist mindset
Joined
Apr 25, 2017
Messages
2,815
Reaction score
6,279
  • Apr 26, 2017
  • #2


I think Star Frontiers / Alpha Dawn had the most effective array of aliens in its original incarnation. They were memorable, covered the most essential sci-fi alien stereotypes while still being distinct, and despite being more than just 'rubber forehead aliens' were easy for players to understand and start roleplaying as.

Aliens added later in the line were not as well-thought out, so prefer not to give them any more thought than their authors did.

I also really dug the Sesheyans from Alternity's Star*Drive setting. Flying player characters are not as hard to deal with in sci-fi, and these nocturnal bat-people were ideal for filling that niche. I especially like the elongated non-humanoid head.

Too bad the rest of Alternity's aliens were serviceable but not terribly inspiring.

 
Reactions: Silverlion, Bunch, Voros and 2 others

I also really dug the Sesheyans from Alternity's Star*Drive setting. Flying player characters are not as hard to deal with in sci-fi, and these nocturnal bat-people were ideal for filling that niche. I especially like the elongated non-humanoid head.

Too bad the rest of Alternity's aliens were serviceable but not terribly inspiring.

I like 2300AD's aliens, just the right mix of weirdness, plausibility and space-operaticness. Same with Thousand Suns'.

I once played in a supers game as an alien made of hydrogen plasma held together by a consciousness that manifested itself as magnetohydrodynamic resonance patterns — i.e. living, thinking, singing star-stuff inside a containment suit. Short game but had a ton of fun.

I love the Xenomorphs from the Alien franchise. The first three Alien films were awesome, with Aliens being the best.

Alien: Resurrection and the two Alien vs. Predator movies were god-awful though. Let's hope this new one is good though.

I'm tempted to make an OSR-style campaign or small-scale fan RPG set in the universe of the Alien films (sans Resurrection, though I may be persuaded to include material for the Predator franchise)

Anybody want to help me make an OSR Aliens RPG?

Doc Sammy said:
Anybody want to help me make an OSR Aliens RPG?
Click to expand...

Here's how I'd go about a Space Marines vs. Alien Horrors OSR RPG (with classes, levels, all the D&Disms:
  • A Universe littered with the ruins of dead civilizations, where lurk the aggressive, clearly weaponized alien horrors that wiped them out (turns out the Cosmic Censor had a preference for bioengineered monsters), but also hide amazing alien technology.
  • Once the Transolar Alliance Marine Corps sent reclamation teams across the borders of Human Space to raid these cemetery worlds for alien tech, but with the Alliance in shambles after the Sol-Eridani Civil War, the old reclamation teams have been disbanded and Space Marines turn to freelance work, space piracy or worse.
  • Classes are Space Marine MOSs: Assault/Heavy Weapons, Medic, Technical, Infiltration/Black Ops and Psionicist/Psi Corps.
  • Alien horrors are randomly generated, as different biomes called for different predators.
  • Ancient alien tech is treasure. Some findings repeat themselves across civilizations, meaning some loot is consistent. Some can be used by all PCs, some only by Technicians or Psionicists, some is incompatible with human biology and/or so complex that only governments and megacorps can make sense of it.

One of the things I like about Spelljammer is how it integrated Mindflayers and Beholders so well, it made sense that they were aliens. Neogi were original to SJ I think and great villains. I'm also a fan of the Giff and Giant Space Hamsters. Never got the hate. Kinda odd how self-serious D&D players can be sometime.

Have a White Star Xenomorph:

AC: 4 [16]
Hit Dice: 5
Total Hit Bonus +5
Attacks: Bite (2D10)
Saving Throw: 12
Special: Acid Blood (1D6 damage per round of contact)
Movement: 12
HDE/XP: 6/400

CoC is about the only RPG I can thing of where I like the aliens.

I've read a lot, a lot of SF in my years, RPG aliens come no where near close to the ones in books. I think there was RingWorld RPG at one point so would love the aliens in that. Star Wars and Star Trek also both have fairly lame aliens to me with a few exceptions.

So I just do my own.

  • Jan 30, 2019
  • #24
xanther said:
CoC is about the only RPG I can thing of where I like the aliens.

I've read a lot, a lot of SF in my years, RPG aliens come no where near close to the ones in books. I think there was RingWorld RPG at one point so would love the aliens in that. Star Wars and Star Trek also both have fairly lame aliens to me with a few exceptions.

So I just do my own.
Click to expand...
I think that in order for an alien race to be useful for a RPG, they need three main attributes:
  • Playable - it's OK in literature to have aliens that are just weird or alien, but it doesn't really make for something playable. The alien's psychology has to be sufficiently relatable that somebody can actually play them. If the physiology is too weird then it can interfere with playability as well. For example, Traveller's K'kree are large horse-sized miliant vegan centaurs that insist on travelling in family units. If you wanted to include them as PCs then the mcguffin would pretty much take over the game.
  • Interesting - The alien has to be interesting; there should be something fun about it that makes folks want to play it. Traveller's Vargr are kind of fun in that way.
  • Differentiated - The alien should have at least one mcguffin or contextual role [1] that differentiates it from other races, otherwise it's just clutter. Certainly if you were publishing something they would be just taking up verbiage without necessarily pulling their weight.
Star Wars and other media franchises
A lot of the TV/movie aliens are more cinematic than anything else - there are loads of aliens in Star Wars, but most of them are just extras in rubber suits hanging out in the background. I particularly liked the take on Jawas in Troops, though, and perhaps the best description of Yoda I ever saw was 'An oven mitt with two facial expressions - patronising and condescending.'



Star trek aliens are kind of boring - Leonard Nimoy made a pretty decent fist of Spock as a character but Vulcans really just have logic and pointy ears. Klingons - I'll talk about warrior races below - and Romulans were really just space Romans. Really there were far too many togas on Star Trek, although I suppose it helped to keep the wardrobe bill in check. Most of the others were just single-concept mcguffins to support the story line of the episode. As for Betazoids, let me present Marina Sirtis in far better form then she ever was in TNG. If you thought Carrie Fisher didn't give a fuck ...



'Bugs' in their various forms aren't terribly interesting except as cannon fodder. You could put them in, but then you're subject to the 'Evil Empires' problem.[2]

Literature
Sector General had loads of different aliens; really it was ER in space and the stories mostly revolved around some interesting new species. There were some interesting takes on physiology but most of the alien psychologies were still pretty much single concept - Cinrusskans were diplomatic cowards, Kelgians were brusque and blunt and didn't tell lies etc. As most of the mcguffins in the aliens revolved around their weird physiologies there's not all that much useful material to crib for a role playing game. A bit here and there but most of them are really just rubber suits with strange illnesses and physiologies that were designed to be weird and therefore awkward in some way that makes them a bit awkward for a RPG, compromising playability. I could see Tralthans, Kelgians and maybe Cinrusskans working in a RPG. The stories were quite good though, some of the more memorable space opera I've read.

Schlock Mercenary is another franchise with loads of different aliens. One thing Howard Tayler has done is to follow through with the implications of most of his mcguffinite. While the comic isn't terribly serious, it does warm up after the first 4-5 story arcs and some of the alien races' mcguffins actually get followed through on. Athough the Oafans wouldn't be terribly convenient to play as a PC[3] they are interesting as a concept, and the story arcs around Eina-Afa and the remains of their civilisation were quite clever. They're also the mcguffin for the most elaborate fart joke in the history of science fiction. Schlock is mostly comic relief but the implications of his mcguffinite do get a decent follow through. Amorphs are really a bit overpowered for a RPG but I could see one as a character concept for s supers game. Some of the other aliens - Uniocs, Frelenti, Fobottr, Essperin and so forth - get some interesting treatments although they're still kind of rubber suits with physiological gimmicks. The slight rubber-suitiness of many of the Schlockverse aliens means they would lend themselves fairly readily to translation to a RPG.

Plenty of other sci-fi franchises feature some aliens, but there aren't many with the proliferation of species like those I mentioned above. In some cases the franchise features a small number of species - the prawns in District 9 for example. The Ringworld 'verse has some interesting aliens, but the kzinti and pak are about the only really playable ones. Puppeteers wouldn't be caught dead adventuring unless they were actually crazy like Nessus. Some other franchises (Flinxverse, for example) make a significant mcguffin of their aliens. There are some ideas you could crib from some of these other 'verses but you might have to go over a lot of material to find much of interest.
_____________________________
[1] This could just be something like 'Aboriginal natives of planet X that the party have some mission on' but to be worth devoting any background to there should be some point to having them in the rules or setting.
[2] Evil empires are fine in literature but limited use in a RPG is that they just take up space on the map, becoming effectively no-go areas unless the party are participants in some sort of rebellion a la Star Wars, or maybe smuggling or raiding operations. When, for example, was the last time you saw a major campaign set in Mordor or the Zhodani Consulate. My general suggestion is not to waste too much verbiage or map space unless the empire itself is a major mcguffin in your setting. You'll note that the Zhos sit on the edge of the Spinward Marches map and the Lunar Empire sit on the edge of the map of Prax/Sartar in RQ2.
[3] Large - in some cases very large - organic hydrogen balloons
 
Last edited: Feb 1, 2019
Reactions: Shipyard Locked

Nobby-W

Not an axe murderer
Joined
Oct 7, 2018
Messages
8,198
Reaction score
21,060
  • Jan 30, 2019
  • #25
Deconstructing a few alien tropes

These are some concepts for races that were deconstructions of various stock sci-fi tropes, really asking the question of 'how might this race interact if they actually had to hang out with other species?'

Warrior race
This takes the concept of the warrior race - Kzinti, Klingons, Aslan - and looks at what their original cultures (note the plural) might do when exposed to interstellar scale commercialism. The meiji regime taking over Japan has some parallels, and one can also ask questions like 'What if this culture actually had a lot of cultural influence on interstellar society'? Perhaps they do a nice line in historical period dramas and Galanglic translations are popular with space weaboos.

Maybe they've introduced some of their cultural mores - a tradition of duelling, habitually wearing a traditional dagger as a fashion accessory. Perhaps they are ritually polite and honourable (a sensible way to behave with armed trading partners) and those values spill over into human residents of space influenced by their society.

The culture could be in flux, with the traditional aristocracy being pushed out by new, industrialist progressives. These people might view wearing traditional dress as archaic. This dichotomy could be visible in regions with large expat populations. Rather than a static culture, they might be a society in flux but exerting significant influence on the society around them.

Bear in mind they're not a single culture, but a morass of different cultures, with perhaps one or two dominant ones wielding the most cultural influence. Stereotypes may not apply at awkward moments.

Pirate/space viking race
This is another trope - fractious mercenary/pirate aliens. Space Orks, Vargr, Space Vikings, Mangalores from The Fifth Element. If you're going to have a species like this, what are their motivations and what is their society like? How are they integrating with the rest of society. Are they viewed as outcasts - if so, where are they getting their pirate ships from?

What is their cultural influence on society at large, or are they marginalised like T5E's Mangalores? Why are they so over-represented as mercenaries? Perhaps it's to get off their poverty-stricken shithole of a home planet, and signing up as mercs is they way to do it. Now there are quite a few expats in circulation and they're associated with mercenary and pirate crews in popular culture.

I can't really see a society like this getting their shit together to build a large interstellar empire of their own, especially not in the presence of a large imperial power as a nextdoor neighbour. For my money, the Mangalores, living on the fringes of society, were perhaps the most plausible depiction of this trope. Zorg's 'Be proud of who you are' comment told us something about how they were perceived in the setting.

Aboriginal carnivore race with large expat population
Think Wookiees before Lucasfilm decided to re-cast them as Ewoks. So there's an aboriginal population of carnivorous aliens living in remote forests. Why are there lots of expats - maybe only some of the world is developed and there is still a lot of undeveloped wilderness.[1]

There are civilised gonks and not-so-civilised gonks. Perhaps there are large expat populations who originally left as indentured labour to help build colonies. Maybe some of the cultural mores of the expats aren't so far from their origin. They're not into cannibalism but don't really consider eating other sophonts to fall in that category. The gonks are marginalised, viewed as itinerant labour or barely civilised savages in some places. In other places they are better integrated into society. They still don't like losing games of holochess.

Out in the back blocks, the uncivilised ones are quite happy with their stone age culture and don't really appreciate interlopers - especially gonk pelt hunters and the crews of the logging outposts and mines. Their natural IR camouflage means they don't show up well on thermal imaging equipment, and they've been known to trade with smugglers to get some high-tech weapons to shoot back at the hunters. In practice they're pretty effective with just their homebrew stone age kit.[2]

Race with parasitoid larval stage
Xenomorphs, prawns, broos all need a live host to breed. What might such a race look like if it was a part of interstellar society. This isn't really a deconstruction of the trope but some ideas about what a species of this type might look like.

The species rose to intelligence through needing to farm and look after its hosts. They have a quasi-religious reverence for their hosts (even though the birthing process usually kills the host), which does spill over into religion. Various cultures of the species have different takes on this, but reverence for their hosts is a part of all religions.

In this case, they managed to accidentally let loose a biological warfare agent (which may or may not have had highly corrosive blood) on their home planet, rendering it uninhabitable. The species survived in orbital space habitats, but had to lease land for breeding stock ranches off nearby human populations, putting the more disgusting aspects of their life cycle front and centre. Brood stage individuals (the species is hermaphroditic) travel to these ranches, impregnate their host with an ovipositor and help look after the herds. As well as a part of the life cycle, most cultures view this as a sort of religious pilgrimage.

The species has flexibile genetics, taking on bits of odd genetic material to fool the host's natural immune system. This tends just to manifest itself in details of protein structures but is the subject of various cults of quack pseduoscience. Some of these cults hold that sentient hosts are best, and there are rumours of pirate raids kidnapping hosts from remote regions. The authorities know what a diplomatic clusterfuck this would be if it got widespread and clamp down on these cults. Nonetheless they manage to survive in the fringes, and with a sort of swing to far right politics in some polities, the doctrine is starting to gain some respectability. This may or may not result in a war.

Ancients
How did the ancients destroy their society? Traveller did one take , Norton's Forerunner stories didn't really explain how. Here's another take on it.

Fermi's paradox can be loosely explained by winding the probabilities right down. What if there were exactly two instances of sophont races emerging naturally in Andromeda and zero in the Milky Way. The first race built a galactic civilisaion and exploited resources until the whole galaxy was an exhaused husk. Some populations of primitive descendents still remain.

The second race had to build a galactic civilisation on a shoestring and were very conservative with resources as most of the worlds had been mined out. Nonetheless they did reach a state of enlightened singularity and embarked on an uplift programme of some promising species. Black, oblong monitoring stations soon become dotted around the galaxy, keeping track of their uplift programmes. As they matured, the species were integrated into the society and properly indoctrinated with respect for finite resources.

At some point an intergalactic wormhole opened up to the milky way. A fleet of GSV-sized motherships was dispatched through the wormhole. One made it to the other side, but they had screwed up their measurements and collapsed the wormhole. The rest of the fleet were unceremoniously dumped out of hyperspace in intergalactic space with nowhere near the endurance to get back. Their best calculations indicate that the wormhole could be expected to re-open in about 150 million years.

The mothership attempted to complete its mission and started a number of uplift programmes on promising worlds. Unfortunately it wasn't up to the job and the AIs running it decided that it needed to preserve a society to monitor the uplifts. It took some of its service biomorphs and adapted them for sexual reproduction so they could maintain their own societies. These were seeded at strategic points.

Some survived, some didn't. Unfortunately the instincts in the biomorphs favoured their prime directive of continuity over indivdual survival and the colonies tended to go extinct if some disaster befell them. Their descendents can be found in the galaxy, with their strange prime directive.

This also serves as an explanation as to why a whole load of intelligent species should suddenly emerge at the same time with no precedent.
___________________
[1] Planets aren't homogeneous, or perhaps this could be phrased as the question: Does your mcguffin really need to take up a whole star system? Do you really need a forest moon or just a forest? Do you really need a desert planet or just a desert? Do you really need a pleasure planet or just a red-light district next to the starport?
[2] To this day, I think Lucas should have stuck with the local-boy-made-good story for Chewbacca.

In a word, spelljammer.

In few words more words spelljammer races were a weird combination of cool goofy that when it works it totally works. big fan of the "dohwar" space penguins that are door to door salesmen that travel in a spelljammer shaped like a penguin with massive wings. The text goes on to state that the wings do nothing.
spelljammer might not be for everyone but if you like your aliens little more ALF this was the line for you.

I always liked the "truly alien" aliens, such as The Thing, and the Pod People/Body Snatchers.
I find aliens that are intellegent Viruses, Plants, Rocks, etc. are more fun than the age old "green alien chick" trope.
That being said I love Spelljammer and it has Hippo-people.

FeralToaster

Legendary Pubber
Joined
Jan 23, 2019
Messages
853
Reaction score
941
  • Jan 31, 2019
  • #28
quick side note/question aren't "the thing" and pod people essentially the same? an invasive biological horror that steals the forms of other creatures around it. the sole difference being one is in a resouce poor enviroment the other in a rich one. ...

back on point, yes you are right but but you can only really have one "truly alien" being in the campaign otherwise you run into the prometheus problem (the movie not the myth) with player characters just sorta sitting back trying to figure out what's going on rather then deciding on a course of action. which is not to say that tired cliches are good but a more recognizable alien does give the player character more choices then confront/runaway that the "truly alien" does.

in my own humble opinion of course, and even as I wrote that I remembered TNG's Crystalline entity which had some of the best decision moments in all of trek.
 
Reactions: noman

Click to expand...
I can't remember where I read it, but I think that The Thing was actually trying to escape from earth or something. Something to do with its ship getting sucked into earths gravity well and then trapped in the ice millions of years ago.
Completely unrelated but still fun: The winter crew at the Amundsen-Scott South Pole station screen a back to back viewing of the three "Thing" movies after the last plane leaves for the winter.
FeralToaster said:
but you can only really have one "truly alien" being in the campaign otherwise you run into the prometheus problem (the movie not the myth) with player characters just sorta sitting back trying to figure out what's going on rather then deciding on a course of action.
Click to expand...
I agree with this, having too many "truly alien" creatures can lead to a mess of Cthulhian type aliens where the only description of them is "You looked at it and now you've gone mad the end"
I like to have a kind of compromise between the two. Bizarre physiology but recognizable psychology or vice versa.
That way you can still have weird and wonderful Aliens without hurting your head.
 

Voros

Giganotosaurus said:
Never heard of it, what's it about?
Click to expand...
Planet sized alien intelligence trying to communicate telepathically with human explorers and blowing their minds in the process. Much hilarity ensues. George Clooney saves the day.

FeralToaster said:
In a word, spelljammer.

In few words more words spelljammer races were a weird combination of cool goofy that when it works it totally works. big fan of the "dohwar" space penguins that are door to door salesmen that travel in a spelljammer shaped like a penguin with massive wings. The text goes on to state that the wings do nothing.
Click to expand...

Your post made me suddenly realize that Spelljammer has an inbred cousin in the form of the Parodius videogame franchise.

ok I know it's not the crawling eye. whatever it is is very cool though. I give up what is it?
 

3rik

Legendary Pubber
Joined
May 13, 2017
Messages
5,285
Reaction score
10,882
  • Feb 1, 2019
  • #36
It Came From Outer Space (1953) one of many gems by the ever excellent Jack Arnold. I love Jack Arnold films. He didn't make many that feature aliens, though.



I feel this alien, inspite of - or maybe thanks to - its low-tech campiness, would fit right into the Mos Eisley cantina scene in Star Wars.
 
Last edited: Feb 1, 2019
Reactions: Voros and FeralToaster

3rik

  • Feb 1, 2019
  • #37
FeralToaster said:
ok I know it's not the crawling eye. whatever it is is very cool though. I give up what is it?
Click to expand...
The Crawling Eye / The Trollenberg Terror (1958), equally enjoyable though not as well executed as Jack Arnold's work. I don't remember if it was explained that the crawling eyes were of alien origin, just that they were hiding in some kind of radioactive cloud.

well, I'm recalling off the mst3k episode which cut a lot out but I believe it was a radioactive cloud hiding/born of the cloud type thing. where the cloud came from? no idea might as well be space.

Voros

Doomed Investigator
Joined
Sep 23, 2017
Messages
17,538
Reaction score
40,877
  • Feb 3, 2019
  • #39
Giganotosaurus said:
Never heard of it, what's it about?
Click to expand...

Nobby-W said:
Planet sized alien intelligence trying to communicate telepathically with human explorers and blowing their minds in the process. Much hilarity ensues. George Clooney saves the day.
Click to expand...

The original 1970s film is a Russian classic by Tarkovsky, same director who did Stalker based on Roadside Picnic by the Strugatsky Brothers. Very Russian though so approach with caution.

Both films are based on the sf novel Solaris by the great Polish sf author Stanislaw Lem. It is actually more accessible than Tarkovsky's film and spends more time detailing the alien intelligence so I'd recommend starting there.
 

Voros

Doomed Investigator
Joined
Sep 23, 2017
Messages
17,538
Reaction score
40,877
  • Feb 3, 2019
  • #40
3rik said:
The Crawling Eye / The Trollenberg Terror (1958), equally enjoyable though not as well executed as Jack Arnold's work. I don't remember if it was explained that the crawling eyes were of alien origin, just that they were hiding in some kind of radioactive cloud.

Click to expand...

There is a pretty great MST3K episode on this film and The Crawling Eye is one of the forms that IT takes in King's novel (in the book IT often appears as 50s creature feature monsters since it takes on the appearance of what most frightens the kids).
